speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 20th of November, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Miriam Freedman. And this is tape number two. At the end of the last tape, you were talking about your move to Nitra.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Shall I mention my name?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. OK, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not-- if you can just continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, we moved to Nitra, and I told you that we lived in a place called 28 [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] means below the castle. And-- because it was a lovely area, actually, and there was a park nearby. And there was a little hill. On top of the hill there was this castle. And the Jewish activities was in the center area, what is called a vatican-- a school and, as I mentioned, the bakeries, and the synagogue, and yeshivas. Everything was in that area. And I used to go to school there. And I remember quite a bit in those days. Also, I remember my teacher as well-- who was not very inspiring. But we studied some Jewish subjects, the prayers, and mathematics, and a little bit of history. Very little memory, really, of my schooling-- it was always disrupted, somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were the school days long days? Were you in school for many hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think it was 1/2 a day-- 1/2 a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with the rest of your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, rest of the time I used to come home, and we used to play in the yard. We used to share-- about five families-- this little bungalow. It's like a-- in middle was a yard, with a dog, and everybody-- the washing outside. It wasn't very, very smart. It was just terribly basic. But it didn't even have a bathroom at all indoors. It was all outside. And nearby was a park, with a little river called River Nitra. And if it was a nice, sunny day, we will go out and play near the river, and swim. And-- and there were swings, and there were other kind of activities in a park. We used to just like kids playing around-- hide and seek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you remember what time of the year you arrived in Nitra?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was summertime. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, as I said, most of my aunties lived there. And we used to go visiting them quite often. My mother had a close relationship with all her sisters. We used to go from one auntie to another auntie. Only one of them had a son, Robby. And I mentioned him earlier. And there was [NON-ENGLISH] as well, in Hebrew's name. We used to play with him. And we used to play with my neighbors. And, of course, in those days, we still used to go sometime, visit my auntie in a farm. My father had a sister in a little village-- village called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that far away from Nitra?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that wasn't far, either. I-- we used to visit there occasionally. And we used to-- that part of the family we used to see some time, yes. My mother had a closer relation with Giska [NON-ENGLISH], This is one of my father's sisters. Because part of them lived in Hungary. But she lived in Slovakia. And I remember her quite a bit coming to us; and another auntie called Rosi [NON-ENGLISH], who I used to see some time; and Mela [NON-ENGLISH]. This is my father's side. And on my mother's side, Ella, and Lily, Seren, and Hugo, and [NON-ENGLISH] was in England. And [NON-ENGLISH] I've seen him, as well. He may have been living in Nitra, as well, but I didn't see him so often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any shortages of food at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started gradually. Yes. Yes. But my father, of course, did any kind of manual work. And he started to look for any kind of work he can get to keep us together. And I-- I believe he worked, for a while, in a brick factory, as well-- the other side of the river. We started to heard of sort more basic things. Of course, things started to change. And accommodation was very confined. And, yes. Things become gradually worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you share rooms to sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes-- more than that. We only had two rooms in that place, and we were about seven people. So yes, we shared as-- our-- yes-- our room in a-- for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as much as I could describe it. It was-- the street, as I mentioned to you-- at the beginning, there was a grocery, I think, on that little rows of cottages. And then there was a entry gate. And there was this yard. And I remember a vicious dog tied up. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --there was one, two, three families that I remember fairly clearly-- the man who owned the grocery; then next to me was another lady who was quite a good acquaintance of my parents. And in-- we were number three. Possibly, we were only four or more. And there was another woman who was living-- or married to a gentile. She was Jewish. She survived the war till the very end. He was a painter, an artist. And I used to play with the kids in the yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But within your two rooms, can you describe what was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was very, very confined. Yes. I remember a table. And there was all around beds and a wardrobe. And that was the main room. And then there was another room, what was a kitchen, living room. And I remember in a bottom of the kitchen the washer and basin, and a cooker. And I think there must have been some folding beds, because nighttime, my brother and some other people must have sleep in that part. And I must have shared a bedroom with my sister in the other-- the big room. So it was very, very confined.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about toilet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if you know the continental, they used to outside in a-- behind all the cottages, there was sort of a-- a hole in the ground. And it was disinfected from time to time. I remember, that was the toilet. And washing was done in a barrel in the kitchen. And the laundry, my mother used to do outside in a-- in a yard. See, she was very, very busy lady, was doing what as much as she could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change for you again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I think that the neighbors started to call us names-- the neighbor flat. And the children wouldn't play with us. And that was quite a surprise, because we used to play in the street hopscotch and all those kids games-- skipping ropes. I remember we used to jump around like all the children play. They wouldn't-- call us names, started to throw stone at us. And decree started to come. The Jewish people have to wear the Star of David. And my father must have got the information, again, from the Jewish center, the synagogues that he used to attended every day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the scarce of food started to be. And we started very, very basic-- a lot of beans I remember eating at home. So obviously, that was a vegetable, because it was a farm area and not far. In the town there were a lot of farmers, so we had quite a bit of vegetables still. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- Were you still at school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still at school, but quite often was disrupted. And my feeling was, if my father used to come home and discuss it and things with my mother-- I got a memory that I never remember seeing my parents rowing. See, if there was any problem, financial, or psychological, or changes in life, standard of living, they used to go for walks. Because I used to see them going walking quite a lot out, and discussing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they had to probably make serious decisions about us. Are we going to remain as a family together? And are we going to-- what's going to happen? They knew very little.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I believe my sister from Palestine, those days, tried to get us out via the Red Cross. But it-- she wasn't successful. I think that something I heard, that some commotion was going on in that direction, that she tried to do something. And schooling was disrupted. The Star of David was introduced; curfews for Jews, when they can go out. And people start disappearing, and labor camp. People were taking off for labor camp. And my late brother was sent to Hungary with my sister, Moshe and Naomi. We had a large family in Hungary. And at times, there were rumors the situation in Hungary was better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as our family were living there, they send my-- two of them. I can't tell you why they chose them, but my feeling was probably my brother was reaching like age of 16. And he would have been probably be taken away. And how old was this sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think she must have been 14. I can't tell you exactly why she has been chosen. Because I have got another sister, Edith, who was a little bit older than her. But she was sent with my brother to Hungary-- and through a secret journey. Somebody was going to smuggle them out for payment. There were a lot of people doing it those days, this kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were left-- Edith, Gertie, me, and my parents, in that cottage. And my brother, before he was going-- I think the part, actually, why they send him away was young people were beaten up. So you see, Nitra was in a interesting way how it was built.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like, we lived in this street. And then you had to come out from-- to the main street to go down to the vatican. But behind there there was, under the hill-- there was also a hill behind our house. There was a secret sort of passage that we Jews started to walk that way to avoid any mobs. Because there were also some pubs on the main road. And people were drinking, and horse and cars outside. people coming, selling their goods. And they're drunk. And the scapegoat was always a Jew. And, of course, they were aroused.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you being attacked and verbally abused by the non-Jews of that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Myself, I was a mischievous sort of kid. I was really-- had quite high-spirited. I remember it. I hate the idea of having the Star of David. I used to take it off. I didn't keep it always in. I couldn't bear-- I knew that if I'm going to go through the street and having the Star of David, also, they probably knew we were Jewish. But on the other hand, I was quite dark. And there were quite a lot of gypsies in our town. So, also the gypsies have been ill-treated by the German and by the Slovaks. But the one was immediately singled out. The Jews were the-- really the main target first. And the gypsies become afterwards as well. And I was dark. So I somehow, I had an idea that I can get away without the Star of David. But I do know my late brother and father were beaten up quite a lot, 'cause my father was taken-- brought back one day-- been attacked by some more young people. They were hiding in the bushes, and when they passed through, they knew he was a Jew. And probably he kept his Star of David on, anyway. And so they knew that something serious is happening now. And they have to make a decision. So they send my brother away to Hungary with my sister. And my father lived in fear, like everybody else. We started really realizing, but my mother-- sister Ella, had quite a good connection. And strange enough, we had a connection with somebody who was belonged to a Nazi party. But he was still a friend of our family, and he really warned us quite often. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember his name. I would have liked to mention it, but I can't remember his name. And he would occasionally, via my aunt, that things-- what to do. And he may have, as far as-- I don't know really. He may have even warned my auntie that my brother should go away. But he helped us-- this man, who was so-called, or he was in a party, Hlinka Party, what was a-- a movement that followed the Hitler ideology. And I think that Tiso-- you probably must have heard about Tiso. He was a priest who was arousing quite a bit the people. He was a real anti-Semite. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --he warned, occasionally, our family. And when, all of a sudden, they start the Jews be sent away to labor camp, we really did not know what to do. And then a decree came, eventually, that all the Jews will be-- have to be assembled, and being transported. And here, some miracle happened. This gentleman warned us. And he-- and he was going to try to save us, because he was a good friend of the family. And even those who were already-- already involved in that movement, did not exactly knew what the consequences are going to be and how the Jews are going to deal-- how they're going to be dealt with. They hadn't an idea-- the final solution-- all these plans of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --on one incident, what I believe happened, all the Jews have to assemble. And we had to go be taken away. And we knew a Jewish doctor who put a decree on the door, on a house. There's a typhoid in this house. And if a typhoid victim-- person will be taken on a transport, it can inflict everybody else with a disease, and it will be a epidemic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened was, is-- my sister Edith supposed to have typhoid. It was a make-believe story-- typhoid. And a big placard had been put in front of our door. So they came to take us away. They've been taken away to the juden-- to the vatican, what is also like a Jewish district. And from there-- from there, they were assembled and decided on which transport to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man was-- possibly the doctor and other people-- managed to persuade the people who were going to-- on a-- on a train, that this girl has got typhoid, and if she goes on there-- with a certificate from the doctor-- if she's going to go on that train, an epidemic will be develop. And this is going to be serious problem. So we had been taken off the train. We had been returned to our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did actually get to the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were all taken back-- my sister, my father, and all of us, five of us, back to our little cottage. So nobody came near us, and life become again-- there were still some Jews. They were, at that time, selecting groups of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just got back to this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the train was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember very much the train. I just remember the area we were taken to there was a terrible commotion and masses of people with suitcases. And to be honest, every time anything like this happened, I always thought it's like we were going on a trip somewhere. And I think I'm-- up till today I still try to block certain memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time I could have been eight-- eight or nine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I left a very important incident that I did not accept. That's going back a bit. I did not accept the fact that I'm different than anybody else. Near us-- I should have mentioned to you when the German actually came into our town. I-- this is a very important incident. I used to visit my aunties very often from our house. My auntie Ella and Miklos, who lived in a very nice, better area than we did, I used to go quite a bit visiting them and do some shopping-- not real shopping, but on the way get some milk or something for them. I used to like going there. There's always some goodies as a child. And they had no children. And they liked me, both, very much. And, on one or two incidents, when I was walking to my auntie, I took a Star of David, and I used to see the gendarmerie, or the Hlinka army passing. And they used to always, have a pencil-- a pen-- examining if this is sewn on. But mine was never sewn on, and I used to just take it off. And, looking back, I can't believe it, that a child had so much courage not to put the Star of David. And then, on one occasion, my auntie asked me to go to grocery, and buy this-- didn't like to go any more-- to get some milk and cheese. And they really cursed me there-- grocery-- filthy, dirty Jew. Things like that I experienced an awful lot. But, strange enough, I must have been kind of a dream child wandering around. Because when the youth movement arrived to Nitra, lorries of young youth, the German youth movement, with a uniform like scouts uniform, arrive our town, I was so elevated, so happy. They were coming. And all the Slovak kids-- and they wave these flags, welcoming the occupation. Really, nobody actually knew what the occupation is. And I was just gazing and looking at them, and quite envious. You know, they look so lovely-- kids like 13, 14-year-old youths, and all blond, and all nicely dressed and singing, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --not realizing at all what it was all about. And to such extent now-- now reached stage that I used to-- couldn't find any friends to play. And I used to go to a park. The park I used to go to play was in Nitra, near us. It was a lovely park. And there, these youngsters used to come, and used to train, or play organized games. And one incident, I went to play on the swing. And I saw this group of kids there. And I was dark, and black eyes. I saw those kids, and I thought, I'm going to play with them. I was quite-- a very daring incident. I never told any member of my family about it. My God, my mother would have walloped me if she would have known what I'm doing. And I went to those kids. And I asked them-- they spoke. I spoke German as well. We spoke German at home. And we just start talking. And they ask me my name. And I said, my name is Eva Mannheimer. And it's a very Germanic name. And they sort of played with me. And they thought-- they just were puzzled that I got dark hair and black eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, how did you got black eyes? And I said, because I forgot to wash them. So I going to go home, and I going to wash them. And it struck me very strongly-- is that I went home, and I probably didn't tell exactly the incident to my mother. But I asked my mother, can I have some soap? And I started to put soap into my eyes. 'Cause I wanted to be like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my eyes become so inflamed. I said, I must wash my eyes-- that I want to have blue eyes-- or something-- so very urgent. And my mother said, no. You're never going to have blue eyes, because you've got the most beautiful black eyes. She was wonderful. She tried to sort of make me feel important, you know, as any mother can. She was a great lady. And this was an incident that I look back, sometime. I said, my God! How daring to do something. I must have been probably eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to play with them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I went back, but they were not there at that time. I used to go there nearly two or three times a day. There were twigs there, and there was nearby ice rink. And in the park, part of the park was turned to ice in wintertime. I don't know. I think I was a solitary figure. I was lot on my own. Also my sister, Gertie, who's near my age-- 11 months difference-- we used to play together. But you know how kids are. We used to play and fight. That's a natural thing between sisters. But we got on. I used to play with my sister quite a lot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was an incident that I remember quite strongly from that time. And then you asked me, later on, how-- about the train. I haven't got-- I just remember that we were there and sort of shuffled, a lot of people. And all of a sudden, we were taken away--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and taken back to our place. When you were taken, who were you taken by?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there came, kind of a gendarmerie, they used to call them, Hlinka. They were sort of soldiers with arms, and they got orders to take us. And this man came and interfered and managed to get us out with a letter of the doctor. So went back to our cottage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from a while, things become as it was before. No schooling, but we lived there for a little while, till actually, the decree came that all the Jews have to be-- must assemble somewhere. And they will have to be evacuated from the town, or deported. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that decree coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do remember that decree very much-- very strongly. Because my late father went to work. Well, he had to keep us, somehow, going. He went to work. I never been to his workplace, but I believe it was on other side of the river. And there was a panic. I do not know. My sister, Edith, must have found out that something is going on. Because she went out to investigate this terrible panic in town. And she sort of-- through another newspaper, and telephone, and if the radio was on-- we didn't have a radio, anyway-- what was going on. So firsthand information was only to go out and find out yourself. So she went out. And sort of decree came that all the Jews have to be-- assemble now and have to be taken away. And by 6:00, there will be curfew-- no Jews allowed to be in the street anymore. And I remember my father went to work, Edith went out to look what's going on. And my mother, and my sister Gertie and me went out to look for our father. And we went till the river. And there was a little bridge and two men standing there. And my mother said that she would like to go to look for my father, I presume. And these men also said, we don't advise you to go across the river. It will be difficult to come back. I think they gave us some kind of hint.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then my mother went to a place of [NON-ENGLISH], the street that my uncle and aunt supposed to be hiding. She knew about it. My uncle, Miklos, who was a accountant in a mill company, he was already in hiding. So they must have told something to my mother-- not to me-- because my mother went to look for them. So we're walking up and down that street of the building. And my mother was-- somebody shouted, Olga, Olga, Olga. And it was her sister, Ella, and she called us in. So we went in. They already were in so-called hiding. They knew what's going on. And I do not know. Maybe they told my mother, or maybe they didn't, or my mother must have known, obviously, something. And my father was not anywhere to be seen. So my sister, Gertie, me, and my mother went into the room. And there we found Auntie Seren, and Lily with her son Robby, and Miklos. And we were eight people in that room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, the loud speakers-- the loud speakers are coming, and tremendous panic. But even then, the time when my mother went to look for my father-- because feel the panic in the street already. Because people were dressed up peculiarly. Some were dressed like Germans. And some took the beard off, although not many people had beard, anyway, by that time. And running around, because Jews had to be assembled, and be taken away, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went in that building. And we did not know how long this was going to happen. But we looked of the window. We could see pregnant women be taken away and beaten. At this time already, we could see panic-- next day. And we did not know where my-- our father was. Did you have anything with you from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just, I can't really understand my mother. What we wore, that what we had. And she may had a little bag of something, but I can't recall any of it. Maybe she took some money, or the possession, whatever she had important, but not like clothes. We didn't have anything at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a possibility that she thought that you would be going home-- and not going straight into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think she previous thought at all we're going to go to hiding. She probably went in there to see what's going on. And then that's the way it happened. We stayed there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We've just reached the end of the second tape, so we have to have a short break.
